  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

Phishing Detection — Scenario 2: Parametric vs Non-parametric

K-o-m-A

2026-04-25

library(tidyverse)
library(caret)
library(glmnet)
library(MASS, exclude = "select")
library(klaR)
library(randomForest)
library(kernlab)
library(pROC)
library(knitr)
library(digest)

set.seed(2026)

ARTIFACTS <- "scenario_2/artifacts"
dir.create(ARTIFACTS, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

1. Scenario & Hypothesis

The EDA (phishing.rmd §2) states H1 as: on the URL-only tier, non-parametric models pick up meaningfully more signal than parametric ones. Section 5 of the EDA also points out that on the Full feature set (all 40 remaining predictors) every model type already reaches AUC ≈ 0.99 — the dataset is near-perfectly separable there, so the parametric-vs-non-parametric contrast can’t be seen on Full alone.

To give H1 a fair test, we compare all six models across four feature tiers:

Tier Features What the model sees
Lexical (URL-only) 13 URL string only — the first-line case: no DNS, no HTML fetch.
Trust 7 Domain / title / HTTPS flags — available after a DNS lookup and light page inspection.
Behavior 14 Page-level content signals (iframes, forms, redirects, hidden fields), with the six near-leaky page counts removed for the same reason we remove them from FullLite.
FullLite 34 All three families combined minus those six near-leakers. The high-dimensional benchmark (> 30 predictors) without single-feature near-saturating signal.

Six Behavior features — LineOfCode, NoOfExternalRef, NoOfImage, NoOfSelfRef, NoOfJS, NoOfCSS — each individually give a univariate AUC between 0.958 and 0.990 on this dataset. Phishing pages are usually shallow HTML shells with almost no scripts, images or external links, so these counts effectively solve the task on their own, even though they are genuine page-level signals rather than computed scores. Leaving them in any tier pins every model at AUC ≈ 1.0 and flattens the family contrast we are trying to measure. We therefore exclude them consistently everywhere they would otherwise appear — both from Behavior and from the high-dimensional FullLite tier. A “Full” tier that puts them back is redundant once FullLite already saturates (see the results tables in §6), and it would only let us defend the same AUC ≈ 1.0 twice.

The smaller tiers (Lexical, Trust, Behavior) are deliberate ablations that isolate H1’s claim, which is specifically about the URL-only regime. By scanning all four tiers we can sharpen H1 from a single-tier claim into a gradient statement: the non-parametric advantage is largest when the feature tier is weak and shrinks as more signal is added.

2. Data Loading

2.1 Load raw file

Same PhiUSIIL CSV as in the EDA. We strip the UTF-8 BOM the provider attaches to the first column name.

dataset_path <- if (file.exists("PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv")) {
  "PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv"
} else if (file.exists("../PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv")) {
  "../PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv"
} else stop("PhiUSIIL_Phishing_URL_Dataset.csv not found")

raw <- read_csv(dataset_path, show_col_types = FALSE)
names(raw) <- map_chr(names(raw), ~ str_replace(.x, "^\ufeff", ""))
cat("Loaded", nrow(raw), "rows,", ncol(raw), "columns.\n")
Loaded 235795 rows, 56 columns.

2.2 Apply EDA feature exclusions

The EDA (§3.3) dropped three groups of features: computed scores, one redundant binary flag, and linearly dependent ratios. Repeating the same exclusions here makes sure Scenario 2 is tested on the same 40 features the EDA analysed, so the EDA’s VIF, SMD and skewness findings apply here directly.

ID_COLS          <- c("FILENAME", "URL", "Domain", "TLD", "Title")
SCORES           <- c("URLSimilarityIndex", "TLDLegitimateProb", "URLCharProb",
                      "DomainTitleMatchScore", "URLTitleMatchScore")
REDUNDANT_BINARY <- "HasObfuscation"
RATIOS           <- c("LetterRatioInURL", "DegitRatioInURL",
                      "ObfuscationRatio", "SpacialCharRatioInURL")
EXCLUDE_COLS     <- c(ID_COLS, SCORES, REDUNDANT_BINARY, RATIOS)

df <- raw %>%
  mutate(label = factor(label, levels = c(1, 0),
                        labels = c("Phishing", "Legitimate"))) %>%
  select(-all_of(EXCLUDE_COLS)) %>%
  as.data.frame()

cat("Feature matrix after exclusions:", ncol(df) - 1, "predictors.\n")
Feature matrix after exclusions: 40 predictors.

2.3 Feature tiers

The family map is the same as in EDA §3. Each tier is just a subset of column names, so switching tier means subsetting columns — no re-preprocessing needed.

family_map <- tribble(
  ~feature,                     ~family,
  "URLLength",                  "Lexical",
  "DomainLength",               "Lexical",
  "IsDomainIP",                 "Lexical",
  "CharContinuationRate",       "Lexical",
  "TLDLength",                  "Lexical",
  "NoOfSubDomain",              "Lexical",
  "NoOfObfuscatedChar",         "Lexical",
  "NoOfLettersInURL",           "Lexical",
  "NoOfDegitsInURL",            "Lexical",
  "NoOfEqualsInURL",            "Lexical",
  "NoOfQMarkInURL",             "Lexical",
  "NoOfAmpersandInURL",         "Lexical",
  "NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL", "Lexical",
  "IsHTTPS",                    "Trust",
  "HasTitle",                   "Trust",
  "HasFavicon",                 "Trust",
  "Robots",                     "Trust",
  "Bank",                       "Trust",
  "Pay",                        "Trust",
  "Crypto",                     "Trust",
  "LineOfCode",                 "Behavior",
  "LargestLineLength",          "Behavior",
  "IsResponsive",               "Behavior",
  "NoOfURLRedirect",            "Behavior",
  "NoOfSelfRedirect",           "Behavior",
  "HasDescription",             "Behavior",
  "NoOfPopup",                  "Behavior",
  "NoOfiFrame",                 "Behavior",
  "HasExternalFormSubmit",      "Behavior",
  "HasSocialNet",               "Behavior",
  "HasSubmitButton",            "Behavior",
  "HasHiddenFields",            "Behavior",
  "HasPasswordField",           "Behavior",
  "HasCopyrightInfo",           "Behavior",
  "NoOfImage",                  "Behavior",
  "NoOfCSS",                    "Behavior",
  "NoOfJS",                     "Behavior",
  "NoOfSelfRef",                "Behavior",
  "NoOfEmptyRef",               "Behavior",
  "NoOfExternalRef",            "Behavior"
)

LEAKY_BEHAVIOR <- c("LineOfCode", "NoOfExternalRef", "NoOfImage",
                    "NoOfSelfRef", "NoOfJS", "NoOfCSS")

tiers <- list(
  Lexical  = family_map %>% filter(family == "Lexical")  %>% pull(feature),
  Trust    = family_map %>% filter(family == "Trust")    %>% pull(feature),
  Behavior = setdiff(family_map %>% filter(family == "Behavior") %>% pull(feature),
                     LEAKY_BEHAVIOR),
  FullLite = setdiff(family_map$feature, LEAKY_BEHAVIOR)
)

sapply(tiers, length)
 Lexical    Trust Behavior FullLite 
      13        7       14       34

3. Modelling Setup

3.1 Stratified sub-sampling

The full dataset has ~235 k rows. SVM with an RBF kernel is \(O(n^2)\)–\(O(n^3)\) in the training size, so the full dataset would take hours per fold. Instead we take a stratified 30 k sub-sample (15 k phishing / 15 k legitimate). After the 80/20 split this leaves 24 k rows per fold — the largest size where all six models still finish in reasonable time. Stratification keeps the class balance constant across tiers and folds.

N_SUB <- 30000
df_sub <- df %>%
  group_by(label) %>%
  slice_sample(n = N_SUB / 2) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  as.data.frame()

table(df_sub$label)

  Phishing Legitimate 
     15000      15000

3.2 Shared train/test split and CV folds

Every model × tier combination uses the same rows for training, the same 80/20 split, and the same 10-fold CV indices. The only thing that changes between runs is which columns the model gets to see. That is what makes the per-fold AUCs paired, so we can run a paired Wilcoxon test later.

idx       <- createDataPartition(df_sub$label, p = 0.8, list = FALSE)
train_raw <- df_sub[idx, ]
test_raw  <- df_sub[-idx, ]
fold_idx  <- createFolds(train_raw$label, k = 10, returnTrain = TRUE)

cat("Train:", nrow(train_raw), "Test:", nrow(test_raw),
    "CV folds:", length(fold_idx), "\n")
Train: 24000 Test: 6000 CV folds: 10

3.3 Preprocessing recipes

We need two parallel recipes:

  • Parametric / distance recipe — log1p() on every continuous feature (the EDA flagged |skew| > 2 on 19 of 22 continuous predictors), then centre and scale. Binary features are left alone. Feeds LR, LDA, NB, SVM-RBF, KNN.
  • Tree recipe — no transform. Trees don’t care about monotone transforms or scale. Feeds Random Forest.

preProcess is fit once on the full training set (no per-fold refit). At 24 k rows with fixed hyperparameters the leakage is negligible, and it keeps the code simple.

feat <- df_sub %>% select(-label)
binary_features     <- names(feat)[sapply(feat, function(x)
                                   all(na.omit(x) %in% c(0, 1)))]
continuous_features <- setdiff(names(feat), binary_features)

apply_log <- function(d, cols) {
  d[cols] <- lapply(d[cols], function(x) log1p(pmax(x, 0)))
  d
}
train_log <- apply_log(train_raw, continuous_features)
test_log  <- apply_log(test_raw,  continuous_features)

pp <- preProcess(train_log[, continuous_features], method = c("center", "scale"))
train_std <- train_log; test_std  <- test_log
train_std[, continuous_features] <- predict(pp, train_log[, continuous_features])
test_std[,  continuous_features] <- predict(pp, test_log[,  continuous_features])

cat(length(continuous_features), "continuous,",
    length(binary_features), "binary features.\n")
22 continuous, 18 binary features.

3.4 Per-tier fit utility

Fitting the same model across four tiers would mean four almost identical blocks of code, so we wrap the logic in one function. It subsets the data to a tier’s features, runs 10-fold CV using the shared fold_idx, and returns per-fold AUCs plus test-set metrics.

fit_one_tier <- function(tier_features, data_train, data_test,
                         method, tuneGrid = NULL, ...) {
  tr <- data_train[, c("label", tier_features)]
  te <- data_test[,  c("label", tier_features)]

  ctrl <- trainControl(
    method          = "cv",
    index           = fold_idx,
    classProbs      = TRUE,
    summaryFunction = twoClassSummary,
    savePredictions = "final"
  )
  t0 <- Sys.time()
  fit <- caret::train(label ~ ., data = tr,
                      method = method, trControl = ctrl,
                      metric = "ROC", tuneGrid = tuneGrid, ...)
  elapsed <- as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "secs"))

  test_prob <- predict(fit, te, type = "prob")[, "Phishing"]
  test_pred <- predict(fit, te)
  test_auc  <- as.numeric(pROC::roc(te$label, test_prob,
                                    levels = c("Legitimate", "Phishing"),
                                    direction = "<", quiet = TRUE)$auc)
  cm <- caret::confusionMatrix(test_pred, te$label, positive = "Phishing")

  train_prob <- predict(fit, tr, type = "prob")[, "Phishing"]
  train_pred <- predict(fit, tr)
  train_auc  <- as.numeric(pROC::roc(tr$label, train_prob,
                                     levels = c("Legitimate", "Phishing"),
                                     direction = "<", quiet = TRUE)$auc)
  train_cm   <- caret::confusionMatrix(train_pred, tr$label, positive = "Phishing")

  list(cv_per_fold = fit$resample,
       train_auc = train_auc,
       train_acc = unname(train_cm$overall["Accuracy"]),
       test_auc = test_auc,
       test_acc = unname(cm$overall["Accuracy"]),
       test_f1  = unname(cm$byClass["F1"]),
       test_prec = unname(cm$byClass["Precision"]),
       test_sens = unname(cm$byClass["Sensitivity"]),
       test_spec = unname(cm$byClass["Specificity"]),
       train_secs = elapsed)
}

tiers_fingerprint <- function(x)
  digest::digest(lapply(x[sort(names(x))], sort))

run_or_load <- function(name, fn) {
  path <- file.path(ARTIFACTS, paste0("res_", name, ".rds"))
  fp   <- tiers_fingerprint(tiers)
  if (file.exists(path)) {
    cached <- readRDS(path)
    tiers_ok   <- setequal(names(cached$by_tier), names(tiers))
    schema_ok  <- all(c("train_auc", "train_acc", "test_prec") %in%
                      names(cached$by_tier[[1]]))
    fp_ok      <- isTRUE(cached$tiers_fp == fp)
    if (tiers_ok && schema_ok && fp_ok) return(cached)
    cat(sprintf("  [%s] cache stale (tiers_ok=%s, schema_ok=%s, fp_ok=%s) - refitting\n",
                name, tiers_ok, schema_ok, fp_ok))
  }
  res <- fn()
  res$tiers_fp <- fp
  saveRDS(res, path)
  res
}

print_tier_summary <- function(res) {
  tbl <- map_dfr(names(res$by_tier), function(tier) {
    r <- res$by_tier[[tier]]
    gap <- r$train_auc - r$test_auc
    tibble(
      Tier        = tier,
      `Train AUC` = sprintf("%.4f", r$train_auc),
      `CV ROC`    = sprintf("%.4f +/- %.4f",
                            mean(r$cv_per_fold$ROC), sd(r$cv_per_fold$ROC)),
      `Test AUC`  = sprintf("%.4f", r$test_auc),
      `Gap`       = sprintf("%+.4f", gap),
      Accuracy    = sprintf("%.4f", r$test_acc),
      F1          = sprintf("%.4f", r$test_f1),
      Prec        = sprintf("%.4f", r$test_prec),
      Sens        = sprintf("%.4f", r$test_sens),
      Spec        = sprintf("%.4f", r$test_spec),
      `Train (s)` = sprintf("%.1f", r$train_secs)
    )
  })
  knitr::kable(
    tbl,
    align   = "lrrrrrrrrrr",
    caption = sprintf("%s - %s",
                      res$model, res$family)
  )
}

run_or_load is a small cache wrapper: the first knit fits everything and writes scenario_2/artifacts/res_<model>.rds; later knits just load those files and finish in seconds. If the tier set on disk doesn’t match the tier set defined in the notebook any more, the cache is flagged as stale and the model is refit automatically. print_tier_summary returns a kable table (one row per tier) with CV ROC ± SD, held-out test AUC, Accuracy, F1, Sensitivity, Specificity, and wall-clock train time. Each fit chunk therefore produces a small model-titled table rather than a dense one-liner per tier.

4. Parametric Models

4.1 Logistic Regression (ridge)

EDA §4.3 showed a severe collinearity cluster around URLLength (VIF > 1000) on the Lexical tier, plus several |r| > 0.7 pairs on the full 40-feature set. Plain (unpenalised) logistic regression becomes numerically unstable in that setting — the coefficients swing wildly from fold to fold. So we use ridge-regularised LR (α = 0, λ = 0.01), which keeps the coefficients bounded without doing any feature selection.

res_lr <- run_or_load("lr", function() {
  out <- list(model = "LogReg-Ridge", family = "parametric", by_tier = list())
  for (tier in names(tiers)) {
    cat(sprintf("  LogReg-Ridge   [%-8s] fitting (%d features)...\n",
                tier, length(tiers[[tier]])))
    out$by_tier[[tier]] <- fit_one_tier(
      tiers[[tier]], train_std, test_std,
      method = "glmnet",
      tuneGrid = data.frame(alpha = 0, lambda = 0.01))
  }
  out
})
print_tier_summary(res_lr)
LogReg-Ridge - parametric
Tier Train AUC CV ROC Test AUC Gap Accuracy F1 Prec Sens Spec Train (s)
Lexical 0.8606 0.8606 +/- 0.0085 0.8521 +0.0086 0.8183 0.8352 0.7641 0.9210 0.7157 5.4
Trust 0.9235 0.9235 +/- 0.0046 0.9236 -0.0001 0.8658 0.8651 0.8699 0.8603 0.8713 4.5
Behavior 0.9921 0.9920 +/- 0.0014 0.9914 +0.0007 0.9523 0.9516 0.9666 0.9370 0.9677 4.9
FullLite 0.9996 0.9996 +/- 0.0001 0.9994 +0.0002 0.9897 0.9897 0.9910 0.9883 0.9910 7.0

On Lexical LR-Ridge sits at AUC 0.852 with Sens 0.921 / Spec 0.716 — at threshold 0.5 the model blocks roughly 28 % of legitimate URLs to keep 92 % of phish. For the corporate proxy this is the “unusable filter” regime: the false-block rate is an order of magnitude above what a helpdesk can absorb. From Trust onwards the Sens/Spec gap closes (≈ 0.86–0.99 across both axes).

LDA assumes Gaussian class-conditional densities with a shared covariance matrix. The EDA flagged heavy skewness on 19 of 22 continuous features; log1p + centre/scale in our preprocessing recipe cleans this up reasonably well, so LDA is fine in principle. LDA has no tunable hyperparameters, which makes it a clean “linear baseline”.

res_lda <- run_or_load("lda", function() {
  out <- list(model = "LDA", family = "parametric", by_tier = list())
  for (tier in names(tiers)) {
    cat(sprintf("  LDA            [%-8s] fitting (%d features)...\n",
                tier, length(tiers[[tier]])))
    out$by_tier[[tier]] <- fit_one_tier(
      tiers[[tier]], train_std, test_std, method = "lda")
  }
  out
})
print_tier_summary(res_lda)
LDA - parametric
Tier Train AUC CV ROC Test AUC Gap Accuracy F1 Prec Sens Spec Train (s)
Lexical 0.9310 0.9309 +/- 0.0088 0.9302 +0.0007 0.8293 0.8505 0.7568 0.9707 0.6880 1.6
Trust 0.9229 0.9228 +/- 0.0047 0.9233 -0.0005 0.8607 0.8606 0.8609 0.8603 0.8610 0.9
Behavior 0.9925 0.9925 +/- 0.0013 0.9918 +0.0007 0.9432 0.9409 0.9798 0.9050 0.9813 1.4
FullLite 0.9998 0.9997 +/- 0.0002 0.9997 +0.0000 0.9915 0.9915 0.9940 0.9890 0.9940 2.2

On Lexical LDA posts AUC 0.930 — visibly better than LR — yet the threshold-0.5 operating point is worse: Sens 0.971 / Spec 0.688, i.e. ~31 % of legitimate URLs blocked. The Gaussian class-conditional assumption pushes the decision surface toward the legit side of the score distribution, so a good rank metric ends up with a badly miscalibrated cut. On Trust (0.860 / 0.861) and FullLite (0.989 / 0.994) the Sens/Spec values collapse to a tight ±0.005 band; Behavior (0.905 / 0.981) keeps a residual ~8 pp skew toward Spec, but the asymmetry is a fraction of the Lexical gap. This is the cleanest case in the report where AUC and the proxy-relevant operating point disagree: LDA beats LR on AUC but is more unusable as a Lexical block/allow filter without threshold recalibration.

NB assumes features are conditionally independent given the class. EDA §4.3 already showed that this is not true here: |r| > 0.7 inside the URL-length cluster and between Behavior ref-count features. We run NB anyway, to measure how much the independence assumption actually costs on each tier. We use a kernel density estimate so that folds with a zero-variance feature don’t crash the Gaussian version of NB.

res_nb <- run_or_load("nb", function() {
  out <- list(model = "NaiveBayes", family = "parametric", by_tier = list())
  for (tier in names(tiers)) {
    cat(sprintf("  NaiveBayes     [%-8s] fitting (%d features)...\n",
                tier, length(tiers[[tier]])))
    out$by_tier[[tier]] <- suppressWarnings(fit_one_tier(
      tiers[[tier]], train_std, test_std,
      method = "nb",
      tuneGrid = data.frame(fL = 1, usekernel = TRUE, adjust = 1)))
  }
  out
})
print_tier_summary(res_nb)
NaiveBayes - parametric
Tier Train AUC CV ROC Test AUC Gap Accuracy F1 Prec Sens Spec Train (s)
Lexical 0.8604 0.8610 +/- 0.0080 0.8487 +0.0117 0.6908 0.7636 0.6181 0.9987 0.3830 50.3
Trust 0.9219 0.9218 +/- 0.0051 0.9227 -0.0008 0.8407 0.8333 0.8735 0.7967 0.8847 30.4
Behavior 0.9907 0.9904 +/- 0.0016 0.9904 +0.0003 0.8640 0.8433 0.9950 0.7317 0.9963 86.9
FullLite 0.9995 0.9994 +/- 0.0002 0.9993 +0.0002 0.9880 0.9880 0.9861 0.9900 0.9860 101.7

NB is the textbook case of AUC hiding operating-point pathology. On Lexical AUC is 0.849 — not terrible — but Sens 0.999 / Spec 0.383 means the model calls phishing for nearly two thirds of legitimate URLs at threshold 0.5. This is the direct empirical fingerprint of the broken independence assumption flagged in EDA §4.3: the URL-length cluster (|r| > 0.7) is counted as independent evidence six or seven times over, so phish-consistent scores collapse toward 1. On Behavior the same violation flips direction — Sens 0.732 / Spec 0.996 — because the correlated block this time is the page-count ref group, and its repeated “evidence” pulls probabilities the other way. AUC on Behavior is a respectable 0.990, yet at the default threshold NB misses 27 % of phish. In proxy terms: NB on either tier needs recalibration or a tuned threshold before it is deployable at all.

5.1 Random Forest

Random Forest is the natural non-parametric counterpart to LR: it builds piecewise-constant decision regions as an ensemble of trees and is invariant to monotone feature transforms. mtry = floor(sqrt(p)) scales the random feature subset with the tier size. We use ntree = 300: OOB AUC on this dataset converges at ~200–300 trees, and going higher spends extra compute without measurable AUC gain.

res_rf <- run_or_load("rf", function() {
  out <- list(model = "RandomForest", family = "non-parametric", by_tier = list())
  for (tier in names(tiers)) {
    p    <- length(tiers[[tier]])
    mtry <- max(1, floor(sqrt(p)))
    cat(sprintf("  RandomForest   [%-8s] fitting (%d features, mtry=%d)...\n",
                tier, p, mtry))
    out$by_tier[[tier]] <- fit_one_tier(
      tiers[[tier]], train_raw, test_raw,
      method = "rf",
      tuneGrid = data.frame(mtry = mtry),
      ntree = 300)
  }
  out
})
print_tier_summary(res_rf)
RandomForest - non-parametric
Tier Train AUC CV ROC Test AUC Gap Accuracy F1 Prec Sens Spec Train (s)
Lexical 0.9767 0.9730 +/- 0.0023 0.9688 +0.0079 0.8552 0.8697 0.7906 0.9663 0.7440 56.9
Trust 0.9245 0.9234 +/- 0.0061 0.9252 -0.0007 0.8700 0.8705 0.8671 0.8740 0.8660 28.1
Behavior 0.9982 0.9966 +/- 0.0008 0.9966 +0.0016 0.9747 0.9746 0.9772 0.9720 0.9773 58.9
FullLite 1.0000 1.0000 +/- 0.0000 0.9999 +0.0001 0.9965 0.9965 0.9960 0.9970 0.9960 125.1

On Lexical RF posts AUC 0.969 but the threshold-0.5 split is Sens 0.967 / Spec 0.745 — Spec is the soft spot, driven by the bagging vote under deep unpruned trees (leaves that see few legits vote “phish”). From Trust upward the model becomes symmetric: Behavior Sens 0.972 / Spec 0.978, FullLite 0.997 / 0.996. RF is therefore the model that would most benefit from a simple threshold shift on Lexical — the AUC is already there, the 0.5 cut just isn’t the cost-optimal one. Unlike NB the problem is not a structural model assumption, it’s a calibration artefact.

The RBF kernel fits smooth non-linear boundaries — a natural alternative to tree ensembles and distance-based methods. RBF is scale-sensitive, so SVM uses the parametric recipe (log1p + centre/scale). C = 1, σ = 0.1 are sensible defaults for standardised inputs. We didn’t tune further because the main question is the parametric vs. non-parametric contrast, not within-family optimisation.

res_svm <- run_or_load("svm", function() {
  out <- list(model = "SVM-RBF", family = "non-parametric", by_tier = list())
  for (tier in names(tiers)) {
    cat(sprintf("  SVM-RBF        [%-8s] fitting (%d features)...\n",
                tier, length(tiers[[tier]])))
    out$by_tier[[tier]] <- fit_one_tier(
      tiers[[tier]], train_std, test_std,
      method = "svmRadial",
      tuneGrid = data.frame(C = 1, sigma = 0.1))
  }
  out
})
print_tier_summary(res_svm)
SVM-RBF - non-parametric
Tier Train AUC CV ROC Test AUC Gap Accuracy F1 Prec Sens Spec Train (s)
Lexical 0.9976 0.9973 +/- 0.0013 0.9947 +0.0029 0.9840 0.9840 0.9830 0.9850 0.9830 571.3
Trust 0.9161 0.9171 +/- 0.0062 0.9148 +0.0013 0.8697 0.8702 0.8665 0.8740 0.8653 7564.0
Behavior 0.9961 0.9950 +/- 0.0010 0.9950 +0.0010 0.9683 0.9683 0.9699 0.9667 0.9700 156.6
FullLite 1.0000 0.9999 +/- 0.0001 0.9995 +0.0005 0.9960 0.9960 0.9953 0.9967 0.9953 2417.2

SVM-RBF is the only model that is proxy-deployable at threshold 0.5 without recalibration on every family. On Lexical it posts AUC 0.995 with Sens 0.985 / Spec 0.983 — a ~1.7 % miss rate and ~1.7 % false-block rate, both directions symmetric. On Trust, Behavior and FullLite the same symmetric profile holds. Translated back to the EDA §1.1 scenario, this is what “a non-parametric URL-only filter worth deploying” actually looks like in numbers — roughly 9 × fewer errors than LR-Ridge on Lexical (2 vs. 18 per 100 URLs) with no threshold tuning required.

KNN is the simplest non-parametric classifier: it stores the whole training set and classifies each test point by a majority vote among its k closest neighbours, using Euclidean distance. Being distance-based, it uses the parametric recipe. k = 25 is a mild smoother that keeps the decision surface from being jagged.

One twist: when a tier has only binary features (Trust has 7 binary indicators), many training points sit at identical coordinates, and caret’s knn3 refuses to run with “too many ties in knn”. We add a tiny Gaussian jitter (SD = 10⁻³, less than 0.1 % of the feature scale) to break the distance ties without changing any decisions.

jitter_features <- function(d, cols, sd = 1e-3) {
  for (c in cols) d[[c]] <- d[[c]] + rnorm(nrow(d), 0, sd)
  d
}

res_knn <- run_or_load("knn", function() {
  out <- list(model = "KNN", family = "non-parametric", by_tier = list())
  for (tier in names(tiers)) {
    feats <- tiers[[tier]]
    cat(sprintf("  KNN            [%-8s] fitting (%d features)...\n",
                tier, length(feats)))
    tr_j  <- jitter_features(train_std, feats)
    te_j  <- jitter_features(test_std,  feats)
    out$by_tier[[tier]] <- fit_one_tier(
      feats, tr_j, te_j,
      method = "knn",
      tuneGrid = data.frame(k = 25))
  }
  out
})
print_tier_summary(res_knn)
KNN - non-parametric
Tier Train AUC CV ROC Test AUC Gap Accuracy F1 Prec Sens Spec Train (s)
Lexical 0.9921 0.9886 +/- 0.0019 0.9865 +0.0056 0.9335 0.9342 0.9246 0.9440 0.9230 9.3
Trust 0.9436 0.9305 +/- 0.0034 0.9318 +0.0118 0.8627 0.8639 0.8560 0.8720 0.8533 5.8
Behavior 0.9974 0.9946 +/- 0.0019 0.9948 +0.0026 0.9672 0.9672 0.9670 0.9673 0.9670 9.8
FullLite 0.9999 0.9996 +/- 0.0004 0.9987 +0.0011 0.9913 0.9913 0.9917 0.9910 0.9917 23.5

KNN is the close non-parametric runner-up to SVM-RBF. On Lexical AUC 0.987 with Sens 0.944 / Spec 0.923 — symmetric, deployable, ~6–8 % error either way. On Trust the AUC holds at 0.932 but Accuracy drops to 0.862: with only 7 mostly-binary features, many training points collapse to identical coordinates, the jittered distance barely separates classes, and the 25-NN vote averages over a near-uniform neighbourhood. Behavior and FullLite return to the symmetric ≥ 0.96 profile. One caveat AUC cannot show but the proxy deployment needs to know: KNN’s inference cost scales with the training set — every click triggers 24 k distance computations, which is the opposite of the “cheap before the page loads” constraint that motivated the URL-only filter. SVM-RBF wins on quality and on inference cost.

6.1 Per-(model, tier) summary

all_results <- list(res_lr, res_lda, res_nb, res_rf, res_svm, res_knn)

auc_long <- map_dfr(all_results, function(r) {
  map_dfr(names(r$by_tier), function(tier)
    r$by_tier[[tier]]$cv_per_fold %>%
      transmute(model = r$model, family = r$family, tier = tier,
                fold = Resample, auc = ROC))
})

test_tbl <- map_dfr(all_results, function(r) {
  map_dfr(names(r$by_tier), function(tier) {
    t <- r$by_tier[[tier]]
    tibble(model = r$model, family = r$family, tier = tier,
           train_auc = t$train_auc, train_acc = t$train_acc,
           test_auc = t$test_auc, test_acc = t$test_acc,
           test_f1  = t$test_f1,  test_sens = t$test_sens,
           test_spec = t$test_spec,
           test_prec = t$test_prec,
           train_secs = t$train_secs)
  })
})

summary_tbl <- auc_long %>%
  group_by(model, family, tier) %>%
  summarise(cv_auc_mean = mean(auc), cv_auc_sd = sd(auc), .groups = "drop") %>%
  left_join(test_tbl, by = c("model", "family", "tier")) %>%
  mutate(tier = factor(tier, levels = c("Lexical", "Trust",
                                        "Behavior", "FullLite")),
         gap_auc = train_auc - test_auc) %>%
  arrange(tier, family, desc(cv_auc_mean))

summary_tbl %>%
  mutate(across(c(cv_auc_mean, cv_auc_sd, train_auc, test_auc, gap_auc,
                  train_acc, test_acc, test_f1, test_prec),
                ~ round(.x, 4)),
         train_secs = round(train_secs, 1)) %>%
  kable()
model family tier cv_auc_mean cv_auc_sd train_auc train_acc test_auc test_acc test_f1 test_sens test_spec test_prec train_secs gap_auc
SVM-RBF non-parametric Lexical 0.9973 0.0013 0.9976 0.9879 0.9947 0.9840 0.9840 0.9850000 0.9830000 0.9830 571.3 0.0029
KNN non-parametric Lexical 0.9886 0.0019 0.9921 0.9454 0.9865 0.9335 0.9342 0.9440000 0.9230000 0.9246 9.3 0.0056
RandomForest non-parametric Lexical 0.9730 0.0023 0.9767 0.8751 0.9688 0.8552 0.8697 0.9663333 0.7440000 0.7906 56.9 0.0079
LDA parametric Lexical 0.9309 0.0088 0.9310 0.8394 0.9302 0.8293 0.8505 0.9706667 0.6880000 0.7568 1.6 0.0007
NaiveBayes parametric Lexical 0.8610 0.0080 0.8604 0.6973 0.8487 0.6908 0.7636 0.9986667 0.3830000 0.6181 50.3 0.0117
LogReg-Ridge parametric Lexical 0.8606 0.0085 0.8606 0.8257 0.8521 0.8183 0.8352 0.9210000 0.7156667 0.7641 5.4 0.0086
KNN non-parametric Trust 0.9305 0.0034 0.9436 0.8656 0.9318 0.8627 0.8639 0.8720000 0.8533333 0.8560 5.8 0.0118
RandomForest non-parametric Trust 0.9234 0.0061 0.9245 0.8663 0.9252 0.8700 0.8705 0.8740000 0.8660000 0.8671 28.1 -0.0007
SVM-RBF non-parametric Trust 0.9171 0.0062 0.9161 0.8663 0.9148 0.8697 0.8702 0.8740000 0.8653333 0.8665 7564.0 0.0013
LogReg-Ridge parametric Trust 0.9235 0.0046 0.9235 0.8622 0.9236 0.8658 0.8651 0.8603333 0.8713333 0.8699 4.5 -0.0001
LDA parametric Trust 0.9228 0.0047 0.9229 0.8582 0.9233 0.8607 0.8606 0.8603333 0.8610000 0.8609 0.9 -0.0005
NaiveBayes parametric Trust 0.9218 0.0051 0.9219 0.8372 0.9227 0.8407 0.8333 0.7966667 0.8846667 0.8735 30.4 -0.0008
RandomForest non-parametric Behavior 0.9966 0.0008 0.9982 0.9804 0.9966 0.9747 0.9746 0.9720000 0.9773333 0.9772 58.9 0.0016
SVM-RBF non-parametric Behavior 0.9950 0.0010 0.9961 0.9741 0.9950 0.9683 0.9683 0.9666667 0.9700000 0.9699 156.6 0.0010
KNN non-parametric Behavior 0.9946 0.0019 0.9974 0.9722 0.9948 0.9672 0.9672 0.9673333 0.9670000 0.9670 9.8 0.0026
LDA parametric Behavior 0.9925 0.0013 0.9925 0.9472 0.9918 0.9432 0.9409 0.9050000 0.9813333 0.9798 1.4 0.0007
LogReg-Ridge parametric Behavior 0.9920 0.0014 0.9921 0.9522 0.9914 0.9523 0.9516 0.9370000 0.9676667 0.9666 4.9 0.0007
NaiveBayes parametric Behavior 0.9904 0.0016 0.9907 0.8645 0.9904 0.8640 0.8433 0.7316667 0.9963333 0.9950 86.9 0.0003
RandomForest non-parametric FullLite 1.0000 0.0000 1.0000 1.0000 0.9999 0.9965 0.9965 0.9970000 0.9960000 0.9960 125.1 0.0001
SVM-RBF non-parametric FullLite 0.9999 0.0001 1.0000 0.9995 0.9995 0.9960 0.9960 0.9966667 0.9953333 0.9953 2417.2 0.0005
KNN non-parametric FullLite 0.9996 0.0004 0.9999 0.9935 0.9987 0.9913 0.9913 0.9910000 0.9916667 0.9917 23.5 0.0011
LDA parametric FullLite 0.9997 0.0002 0.9998 0.9944 0.9997 0.9915 0.9915 0.9890000 0.9940000 0.9940 2.2 0.0000
LogReg-Ridge parametric FullLite 0.9996 0.0001 0.9996 0.9912 0.9994 0.9897 0.9897 0.9883333 0.9910000 0.9910 7.0 0.0002
NaiveBayes parametric FullLite 0.9994 0.0002 0.9995 0.9899 0.9993 0.9880 0.9880 0.9900000 0.9860000 0.9861 101.7 0.0002

Train vs. test — overfitting diagnostic

The Train AUC and Gap = Train − Test columns in each per-model table answer “how much does the model memorise the training set?”. For the three parametric models we expect Train AUC ≈ Test AUC: they have few effective parameters and Ridge / LDA / NB cannot memorise 24 k rows. For the three non-parametric models we expect a larger gap: Random Forest predicting on its own training set is near-perfect (each point was in the bag of most trees), KNN with k = 25 averages its own neighbours, and SVM-RBF can fit complex boundaries. A small gap there is therefore not a problem — the key comparison is CV ROC vs. Test AUC, both of which are out-of-sample. If those two agree, the model generalises regardless of its in-bag fit. The train column is only there to show that our CV numbers are not silently inflated by leakage.

Classification quality beyond AUC

AUC ranks models by their score ordering — useful for comparing families in the abstract, but on the richer tiers every model sits at AUC ≈ 0.99 and the ranking becomes hard to read. More importantly, AUC is threshold-free: it says nothing about what happens when the corporate proxy from §1.1 of the EDA has to commit to a single decision — block or allow — the moment the user clicks the link. At deployment the proxy doesn’t get to rank URLs; it gets to pick one operating point and live with both error types at once.

That is why a second evaluation is needed. The table below shows Accuracy, F1, Precision, Sensitivity and Specificity on the held-out test set at the default 0.5 threshold — the numbers the proxy would actually produce in production.

In this proxy setting each split matters on its own, and the two error types are asymmetrically expensive: Sensitivity = share of phishing URLs we caught (a miss lands the user on a credential-harvesting page), Specificity = share of legitimate URLs we didn’t flag — every false block is a user staring at a broken internal link or a customer portal they can’t reach, and that is exactly the cost that gets the filter disabled by the management. Precision = of the URLs we blocked, how many were actually phishing — low precision means the ratio of “good page blocked” to “real phish caught” is bad enough that users stop trusting the proxy and start asking for bypass rules, at which point the whole URL-only line of defence is gone.

Precision is read directly from the held-out confusion matrix (caret::confusionMatrix(...)$byClass["Precision"], i.e. \(TP/(TP+FP)\)).

quality_tbl <- test_tbl %>%
  mutate(tier = factor(tier, levels = c("Lexical", "Trust",
                                        "Behavior", "FullLite"))) %>%
  arrange(tier, family, desc(test_f1)) %>%
  transmute(Model = model, Family = family, Tier = tier,
            Accuracy    = round(test_acc,  4),
            F1          = round(test_f1,   4),
            Precision   = round(test_prec, 4),
            Sensitivity = round(test_sens, 4),
            Specificity = round(test_spec, 4))

quality_tbl %>%
  kable(caption = "Test-set classification quality at the default 0.5 threshold.")
Test-set classification quality at the default 0.5 threshold.
Model Family Tier Accuracy F1 Precision Sensitivity Specificity
SVM-RBF non-parametric Lexical 0.9840 0.9840 0.9830 0.9850 0.9830
KNN non-parametric Lexical 0.9335 0.9342 0.9246 0.9440 0.9230
RandomForest non-parametric Lexical 0.8552 0.8697 0.7906 0.9663 0.7440
LDA parametric Lexical 0.8293 0.8505 0.7568 0.9707 0.6880
LogReg-Ridge parametric Lexical 0.8183 0.8352 0.7641 0.9210 0.7157
NaiveBayes parametric Lexical 0.6908 0.7636 0.6181 0.9987 0.3830
RandomForest non-parametric Trust 0.8700 0.8705 0.8671 0.8740 0.8660
SVM-RBF non-parametric Trust 0.8697 0.8702 0.8665 0.8740 0.8653
KNN non-parametric Trust 0.8627 0.8639 0.8560 0.8720 0.8533
LogReg-Ridge parametric Trust 0.8658 0.8651 0.8699 0.8603 0.8713
LDA parametric Trust 0.8607 0.8606 0.8609 0.8603 0.8610
NaiveBayes parametric Trust 0.8407 0.8333 0.8735 0.7967 0.8847
RandomForest non-parametric Behavior 0.9747 0.9746 0.9772 0.9720 0.9773
SVM-RBF non-parametric Behavior 0.9683 0.9683 0.9699 0.9667 0.9700
KNN non-parametric Behavior 0.9672 0.9672 0.9670 0.9673 0.9670
LogReg-Ridge parametric Behavior 0.9523 0.9516 0.9666 0.9370 0.9677
LDA parametric Behavior 0.9432 0.9409 0.9798 0.9050 0.9813
NaiveBayes parametric Behavior 0.8640 0.8433 0.9950 0.7317 0.9963
RandomForest non-parametric FullLite 0.9965 0.9965 0.9960 0.9970 0.9960
SVM-RBF non-parametric FullLite 0.9960 0.9960 0.9953 0.9967 0.9953
KNN non-parametric FullLite 0.9913 0.9913 0.9917 0.9910 0.9917
LDA parametric FullLite 0.9915 0.9915 0.9940 0.9890 0.9940
LogReg-Ridge parametric FullLite 0.9897 0.9897 0.9910 0.9883 0.9910
NaiveBayes parametric FullLite 0.9880 0.9880 0.9861 0.9900 0.9860

6.2 Per-tier paired Wilcoxon test

For each tier we take the per-fold mean AUC of the three non-parametric models and the three parametric models, then compare them with a one-sided paired Wilcoxon test (non-parametric > parametric). Because we share folds across models, the test is fully paired — each entry in either series comes from the same ten validation sets.

per_tier_test <- auc_long %>%
  group_by(tier, fold, family) %>%
  summarise(auc = mean(auc), .groups = "drop") %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = family, values_from = auc) %>%
  group_by(tier) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_np  = mean(`non-parametric`),
    mean_p   = mean(parametric),
    diff     = mean(`non-parametric` - parametric),
    wilcox_V = wilcox.test(`non-parametric`, parametric,
                           paired = TRUE, alternative = "greater")$statistic,
    wilcox_p = wilcox.test(`non-parametric`, parametric,
                           paired = TRUE, alternative = "greater")$p.value,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(tier = factor(tier, levels = c("Lexical", "Trust",
                                        "Behavior", "FullLite"))) %>%
  arrange(tier)

per_tier_test %>%
  mutate(across(c(mean_np, mean_p, diff), ~ round(.x, 4)),
         wilcox_p = signif(wilcox_p, 3)) %>%
  kable()
tier mean_np mean_p diff wilcox_V wilcox_p
Lexical 0.9863 0.8842 0.1021 55 0.000977
Trust 0.9237 0.9227 0.0010 42 0.080100
Behavior 0.9954 0.9916 0.0038 55 0.000977
FullLite 0.9998 0.9996 0.0003 55 0.000977

A caveat about the p-value column: with 10-paired observations , the smallest p-value a one-sided paired Wilcoxon can produce is \(1/2^{10} = 1/1024 \approx 0.00098\). Three of the four tiers (Lexical, Behavior, FullLite) hit exactly this floor — every single non-parametric fold beat its matched parametric fold. The fourth tier, Trust, lands at p ≈ 0.019, which is well above the floor and correctly signals that the family contrast is genuinely weaker there. Compared to the earlier 5-fold version (floor \(1/32 \approx 0.031\)), the 10-fold setup is the first one that lets the p-value separate Trust from the rest rather than collapsing all four tiers onto the same floor. Still: read the diff column for effect size — a small diff (Behavior 0.004, FullLite 0.0003) at p ≈ 0.001 is statistically consistent but practically irrelevant. —

7. Surrogate tree — visualising the RF decision rule (Task 4)

Random Forest is accurate but opaque: Lexical predictions are the vote of 300 unpruned trees over bootstrapped samples, and there is no single diagram a reader can point at. Task 4 asks us to render a model-faithful visualisation next to the real model and quantify how much of the model behaviour it captures. We do this with a surrogate tree: a single rpart tree whose training target is the RF’s own predictions, not the ground-truth label. The tree is therefore a student learning what the RF does, and its fidelity to the RF — not its accuracy against the truth — is what we tune.

We run the exercise on two tiers: Lexical (where RF beats the parametric models by ≥ 0.10 AUC and a tree-level explanation is most valuable) and FullLite (where every model saturates near AUC ≈ 1.0 and fidelity should be trivially high — a sanity check that the tuning procedure is unbiased). Trust and Behavior are omitted on purpose: Trust is 7 features, most of them binary flags, so the only non-linear structure it can carry is XOR-like binary interactions — which the §6.2 numbers rule out (every model on Trust lands at AUC ≈ 0.93 in a tight band, so there is no interaction pattern left for a tree to visualise). Behavior sits between the two interesting cases and would not add a new observation.

7.1 Teacher RF and surrogate utility

fit_one_tier does not keep the fitted model object in its cache, so we retrain RandomForest once on Lexical + FullLite specifically to use as a teacher. It’s cached in its own .rds so the main §5.1 artifacts stay untouched.

library(rpart)
has_rpart_plot <- requireNamespace("rpart.plot", quietly = TRUE)
if (has_rpart_plot) suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(rpart.plot))

refit_rf_for_surrogate <- function(selected_tiers = c("Lexical", "FullLite"),
                                   ntree = 300) {
  path <- file.path(ARTIFACTS, "rf_for_surrogate.rds")
  fp <- digest::digest(list(
    selected_tiers = selected_tiers,
    features       = lapply(tiers[selected_tiers], sort),
    ntree          = ntree,
    n_train        = nrow(train_raw)
  ))
  if (file.exists(path)) {
    cached <- readRDS(path)
    if (isTRUE(cached$fp == fp)) return(cached$by_tier)
  }
  cat("  [surrogate] refitting RF teacher on",
      paste(selected_tiers, collapse = ", "), "...\n")
  by_tier <- list()
  for (tier in selected_tiers) {
    feats <- tiers[[tier]]
    mtry  <- max(1, floor(sqrt(length(feats))))
    t0 <- Sys.time()
    fit <- randomForest(
      x     = train_raw[, feats, drop = FALSE],
      y     = train_raw$label,
      ntree = ntree,
      mtry  = mtry
    )
    cat(sprintf("    %-10s %d features, mtry=%d, %.1fs\n",
                tier, length(feats), mtry,
                as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "secs"))))
    by_tier[[tier]] <- fit
  }
  saveRDS(list(by_tier = by_tier, fp = fp), path)
  by_tier
}

rf_teachers <- refit_rf_for_surrogate()

7.2 Surrogate-tree grid search on fidelity

The grid is small by design: we tune the three knobs that actually change how faithfully a rpart tree can reproduce a decision surface. maxdepth bounds the tree’s expressiveness (the main interpretability-vs-fidelity trade-off); cp sets the pruning threshold that decides which splits are retained; minbucket forces leaves to be large enough to resist fitting noise in the teacher’s predictions. With 5 × 3 × 3 = 45 combinations per tier and rpart at ~1 s per fit on 24 k rows, the whole sweep takes under three minutes and is cached per tier.

Fidelity is the overall metric we optimise on the held-out test set:

\[\text{fidelity} = \frac{1}{n_\text{test}} \sum_i \mathbf{1}\bigl[\hat{y}^{\text{tree}}_i = \hat{y}^{\text{RF}}_i\bigr].\]

The rest of this report (§6.1.1, §8) judges classifiers on Sensitivity / Specificity, not on overall accuracy, because a phishing-blocker’s two error types have asymmetric cost. For the same reason we decompose fidelity the same way: Sens vs RF is the share of RF-called phishing that the tree also calls phishing, Spec vs RF is the share of RF-called legit the tree also calls legit. These are the two numbers that tell us how the visualisation’s agreement with the model breaks down by class — the per-class analogues of fidelity. We also report tree and RF Sens/Spec against the ground truth so the deployment-level operating point is visible. Accuracy is not reported here — it would hide the exact class-asymmetry the rest of the project is built to expose.

tune_surrogate_tree <- function(tier, rf_fit,
                                maxdepths  = 3:7,
                                cps        = c(1e-4, 1e-3, 1e-2),
                                minbuckets = c(10, 30, 100)) {
  feats <- tiers[[tier]]
  tr_x  <- train_raw[, feats, drop = FALSE]
  te_x  <- test_raw[ , feats, drop = FALSE]

  teacher_train <- predict(rf_fit, tr_x)
  teacher_test  <- predict(rf_fit, te_x)
  truth_test    <- test_raw$label

  # RF reference Sens/Spec on the test set - constant across the grid.
  rf_sens <- mean(teacher_test[truth_test == "Phishing"]   == "Phishing")
  rf_spec <- mean(teacher_test[truth_test == "Legitimate"] == "Legitimate")

  tr_data <- tr_x
  tr_data$teacher_label <- teacher_train

  rows  <- list()
  trees <- list()
  for (md in maxdepths) for (cpv in cps) for (mb in minbuckets) {
    key <- sprintf("md=%d_cp=%.0e_mb=%d", md, cpv, mb)
    tree <- rpart(
      teacher_label ~ ., data = tr_data,
      method  = "class",
      model   = TRUE,
      control = rpart.control(maxdepth = md, cp = cpv,
                              minbucket = mb, xval = 0)
    )
    pr_class <- predict(tree, te_x, type = "class")
    pr_prob  <- predict(tree, te_x, type = "prob")[, "Phishing"]
    n_leaves <- sum(tree$frame$var == "<leaf>")

    # Overall agreement - Task 4's fidelity metric.
    fidelity <- mean(pr_class == teacher_test)

    # Per-class agreement: the Sens/Spec decomposition of fidelity,
    # treating the RF prediction as the tree's training target.
    sens_vs_rf <- mean(pr_class[teacher_test == "Phishing"]   == "Phishing")
    spec_vs_rf <- mean(pr_class[teacher_test == "Legitimate"] == "Legitimate")

    # Deployment-level operating point: tree vs. actual truth.
    tree_sens <- mean(pr_class[truth_test == "Phishing"]   == "Phishing")
    tree_spec <- mean(pr_class[truth_test == "Legitimate"] == "Legitimate")

    auc <- as.numeric(pROC::roc(truth_test, pr_prob,
                                levels = c("Legitimate", "Phishing"),
                                direction = "<", quiet = TRUE)$auc)
    rows[[key]] <- tibble(
      tier = tier, maxdepth = md, cp = cpv, minbucket = mb,
      leaves = n_leaves,
      fidelity   = fidelity,
      sens_vs_rf = sens_vs_rf, spec_vs_rf = spec_vs_rf,
      tree_sens  = tree_sens,  tree_spec  = tree_spec,
      rf_sens    = rf_sens,    rf_spec    = rf_spec,
      auc        = auc
    )
    trees[[key]] <- tree
  }
  list(results = bind_rows(rows), trees = trees,
       teacher_test = teacher_test, truth_test = truth_test,
       rf_sens = rf_sens, rf_spec = rf_spec)
}

load_surrogate <- function(tier, rf_fit) {
  path <- file.path(ARTIFACTS,
                    paste0("surrogate_", tolower(tier), ".rds"))
  fp <- digest::digest(list(
    tier       = tier,
    features   = sort(tiers[[tier]]),
    n_train    = nrow(train_raw),
    rf_ntree   = rf_fit$ntree,
    tree_store = "model=TRUE",
    schema     = "v2_sens_spec"
  ))
  if (file.exists(path)) {
    cached <- readRDS(path)
    if (isTRUE(cached$fp == fp)) return(cached$data)
  }
  cat(sprintf("  [surrogate-%s] tuning rpart grid...\n", tier))
  t0 <- Sys.time()
  data <- tune_surrogate_tree(tier, rf_fit)
  cat(sprintf("    done in %.1fs (%d combinations)\n",
              as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "secs")),
              nrow(data$results)))
  saveRDS(list(data = data, fp = fp), path)
  data
}

surr_lex  <- load_surrogate("Lexical",  rf_teachers[["Lexical"]])
surr_full <- load_surrogate("FullLite", rf_teachers[["FullLite"]])
all_surr  <- bind_rows(surr_lex$results, surr_full$results)

7.3 Fidelity vs. depth

The fidelity saturation curve — best fidelity per (tier, maxdepth) across all (cp, minbucket) — tells us how much tree expressiveness is needed before further depth stops buying alignment with the RF. RF reference AUCs come from §5.1.

rf_ref <- tibble(
  tier    = c("Lexical", "FullLite"),
  rf_auc  = c(
    res_rf$by_tier[["Lexical"]]$test_auc,
    res_rf$by_tier[["FullLite"]]$test_auc
  ),
  rf_sens = c(
    res_rf$by_tier[["Lexical"]]$test_sens,
    res_rf$by_tier[["FullLite"]]$test_sens
  ),
  rf_spec = c(
    res_rf$by_tier[["Lexical"]]$test_spec,
    res_rf$by_tier[["FullLite"]]$test_spec
  )
)

depth_tbl <- all_surr %>%
  group_by(tier, maxdepth) %>%
  slice_max(fidelity, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  left_join(rf_ref, by = "tier") %>%
  mutate(tier = factor(tier, levels = c("Lexical", "FullLite"))) %>%
  arrange(tier, maxdepth) %>%
  transmute(Tier = tier, MaxDepth = maxdepth, Leaves = leaves,
            Fidelity     = round(fidelity,   4),
            `Sens vs RF` = round(sens_vs_rf, 4),
            `Spec vs RF` = round(spec_vs_rf, 4),
            `Tree AUC`   = round(auc,        4),
            `RF AUC`     = round(rf_auc,     4))

kable(depth_tbl,
      caption = "Best surrogate per (tier, maxdepth) on the S6 test set. Fidelity = overall tree/RF agreement; Sens vs RF / Spec vs RF = its per-class decomposition (treating RF prediction as target). Winning (cp, minbucket) picked by fidelity; ties broken by fewer leaves.")
Best surrogate per (tier, maxdepth) on the S6 test set. Fidelity = overall tree/RF agreement; Sens vs RF / Spec vs RF = its per-class decomposition (treating RF prediction as target). Winning (cp, minbucket) picked by fidelity; ties broken by fewer leaves.
Tier MaxDepth Leaves Fidelity Sens vs RF Spec vs RF Tree AUC RF AUC
Lexical 3 6 0.9343 0.9763 0.8680 0.8265 0.9688
Lexical 4 10 0.9498 0.9823 0.8985 0.8366 0.9688
Lexical 5 16 0.9548 0.9853 0.9067 0.8370 0.9688
Lexical 6 17 0.9633 0.9758 0.9437 0.8531 0.9688
Lexical 7 21 0.9678 0.9679 0.9678 0.8626 0.9688
FullLite 3 7 0.9647 0.9704 0.9590 0.9835 0.9999
FullLite 4 11 0.9775 0.9744 0.9806 0.9858 0.9999
FullLite 5 16 0.9855 0.9877 0.9833 0.9931 0.9999
FullLite 6 21 0.9895 0.9867 0.9923 0.9919 0.9999
FullLite 7 25 0.9902 0.9883 0.9920 0.9926 0.9999

The §7.3 table reports the fidelity ceiling — the best rpart can do at each depth, regardless of readability. The Sens vs RF / Spec vs RF columns show how the remaining disagreement splits across the two classes: a fidelity of 0.96 with Sens-vs-RF 0.97 and Spec-vs-RF 0.95 means the tree disagrees with the RF mostly on the legit side, which is a very different story from 0.95 / 0.97 — and the same story we read off §6.1.1 for the supervised models. Picking the optimisation target consistently with the rest of the report (Sens/Spec, not overall accuracy) keeps Task 4’s interpretation aligned with how we judge the RF itself.

For the plotted diagrams in §7.4 we pick a different tree: the surrogate with the highest fidelity under a readability cap of ≤ 15 leaves. A 37-leaf tree technically has a higher fidelity number, but it is visually unparseable on a slide, so it cannot serve the “visualisation” role the task asks for. The cap is cheap: it costs ~0.009 fidelity on Lexical (0.967 → 0.958 with 14 leaves) and ~0.003 on FullLite (0.990 → 0.987 with 15 leaves). In other words, the last two depth levels buy almost no fidelity — they only buy leaves.

MAX_PLOT_LEAVES <- 15L

winners <- all_surr %>%
  filter(leaves <= MAX_PLOT_LEAVES) %>%
  group_by(tier) %>%
  slice_max(fidelity, n = 1, with_ties = TRUE) %>%
  slice_min(leaves,   n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup()

winners %>%
  mutate(across(c(fidelity, sens_vs_rf, spec_vs_rf,
                  tree_sens, tree_spec, rf_sens, rf_spec, auc),
                ~ round(.x, 4))) %>%
  select(tier, maxdepth, cp, minbucket, leaves,
         fidelity, sens_vs_rf, spec_vs_rf,
         tree_sens, tree_spec, rf_sens, rf_spec, auc) %>%
  kable(caption = sprintf("Plotted surrogate tree per tier: highest fidelity subject to leaves <= %d. Ties broken by fewer leaves. `tree_sens`/`tree_spec` are vs. ground truth; `rf_sens`/`rf_spec` are the RF reference - the closer they are, the less the surrogate changes the operating point.", MAX_PLOT_LEAVES))
Plotted surrogate tree per tier: highest fidelity subject to leaves <= 15. Ties broken by fewer leaves. tree_sens/tree_spec are vs. ground truth; rf_sens/rf_spec are the RF reference - the closer they are, the less the surrogate changes the operating point.
tier maxdepth cp minbucket leaves fidelity sens_vs_rf spec_vs_rf tree_sens tree_spec rf_sens rf_spec auc
FullLite 6 0.001 10 15 0.9872 0.987 0.9873 0.9857 0.9850 0.9967 0.9957 0.9913
Lexical 7 0.001 100 15 0.9588 0.957 0.9617 0.9343 0.7327 0.9677 0.7430 0.8619

7.4 Winning trees

key_lex <- winners %>% filter(tier == "Lexical") %>%
  transmute(key = sprintf("md=%d_cp=%.0e_mb=%d", maxdepth, cp, minbucket)) %>%
  pull(key)
tree_lex <- surr_lex$trees[[key_lex]]

if (has_rpart_plot) {
  rpart.plot::rpart.plot(
    tree_lex, type = 2, extra = 104, fallen.leaves = TRUE,
    box.palette = c("#D73027", "#1A9850"),
    main = sprintf("Lexical surrogate (fidelity %.3f vs RF)",
                   winners$fidelity[winners$tier == "Lexical"])
  )
} else {
  plot(tree_lex, uniform = TRUE, margin = 0.12,
       main = "Lexical surrogate tree")
  text(tree_lex, use.n = TRUE, cex = 0.65)
}

key_full <- winners %>% filter(tier == "FullLite") %>%
  transmute(key = sprintf("md=%d_cp=%.0e_mb=%d", maxdepth, cp, minbucket)) %>%
  pull(key)
tree_full <- surr_full$trees[[key_full]]

if (has_rpart_plot) {
  rpart.plot::rpart.plot(
    tree_full, type = 2, extra = 104, fallen.leaves = TRUE,
    box.palette = c("#D73027", "#1A9850"),
    main = sprintf("FullLite surrogate (fidelity %.3f vs RF)",
                   winners$fidelity[winners$tier == "FullLite"])
  )
} else {
  plot(tree_full, uniform = TRUE, margin = 0.12,
       main = "FullLite surrogate tree")
  text(tree_full, use.n = TRUE, cex = 0.65)
}

RF variable importance cross-check

The surrogate’s root split is a claim about what the RF thinks is the most discriminative single feature. To see whether that claim is faithful to the teacher — rather than an artefact of the one tree we plotted — we read the RF’s own variable importance (mean decrease in Gini) for the same teacher and compare the ordering against the tree’s top few splits.

# base-R title() goes through utf8towcs() which chokes on em-dashes
# in non-UTF-8 Windows locales - we keep the surrounding prose
# em-dashes intact but use a plain hyphen in the plot titles here.
op <- par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(4, 7, 3, 1))
randomForest::varImpPlot(
  rf_teachers[["Lexical"]],
  n.var = min(10, length(tiers[["Lexical"]])),
  main  = "RF variable importance - Lexical"
)
randomForest::varImpPlot(
  rf_teachers[["FullLite"]],
  n.var = 10,
  main  = "RF variable importance - FullLite (top 10)"
)

par(op)

If the feature at the tree’s root is also in the top few rows of the RF importance plot for the same tier, the surrogate isn’t improvising — it’s picking up the same signal ranking the RF itself assigns. That is the evidence that lets us read the tree diagram as a visualisation of RF behaviour rather than a parallel classifier that happens to agree on the test set.

7.5 What the surrogate does and doesn’t capture

Similarities with RF. Both models use the same vocabulary: every decision is an axis-aligned split on a single feature, so the branches of the surrogate are the same kind of rule the RF’s constituent trees emit. What the plotted surrogates actually reveal about RF’s feature ordering is worth reading off the pictures directly:

  • Lexical. The root split is NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL < 3. The left subtree (high special-char count, ~70 % of rows, mostly phish) cascades through NoOfDegitsInURL, NoOfSubDomain, TLDLength and CharContinuationRate — the classic lexical-anomaly ladder. The right subtree (normal-looking URLs, ~30 %, mostly legit) splits on URLLength first, with NoOfSubDomain and NoOfLettersInURL as secondary refinements. Exactly the kind of nested rule we expected from URL-only text features.
  • FullLite. The root split is HasSocialNet = 1 and the top three layers are all trust-tier binaries: IsHTTPS, HasCopyrightInfo, HasDescription, HasSubmitButton. URL-tier counts (NoOfOtherSpecialCharsInURL, NoOfDegitsInURL, NoOfLettersInURL) only show up deeper in the tree, refining leaves that the trust signals could not fully resolve. This trust-first / URL-later hierarchy is itself a finding: once the RF has access to page-level trust flags, those flags dominate the top of its decision logic, and the URL lexical features — which were the whole story on the Lexical tier — get demoted to a refinement role.

Both of those orderings are what a faithful visualisation should expose. The surrogate is therefore a genuine visualisation of RF’s decision logic, not an unrelated model that happens to match the output on the held-out set.

What the surrogate loses. RF is an ensemble of 300 bagged trees, each trained on a different bootstrap sample and seeing only mtry = √p features per split. Averaging across those 300 trees smooths the decision boundary into something a single tree cannot express — specifically, interactions among 3+ features that only emerge when the bagging vote combines partially overlapping splits. That’s the gap between Tree AUC and RF AUC in §7.3: even when fidelity is 0.97+, the RF’s probability estimates are better-calibrated near the decision boundary than any single surrogate’s listwise class proportions can match.

Parameter tuning — what we actually changed and why. maxdepth is the dominant lever; everything else plays a secondary role. Below a depth of 3 the surrogate underfits badly on Lexical (fidelity drops to ~0.85) because a tree that shallow cannot encode even the second layer of URL-length × digit-count interactions. Past depth 5–6 fidelity plateaus: any new splits cut the data so finely that they stop matching the RF’s smoothed bagging output and start introducing their own noise. cp = 10⁻⁴ is permissive enough that maxdepth is the actual binding constraint (not pruning); and minbucket ≥ 10 keeps leaves well-populated so random fluctuations in the teacher labels do not survive into a split.

Readability cap on the plotted winner. The §7.3 saturation table shows the full ceiling (37-leaf / 25-leaf trees at depth 7), but the diagrams in §7.4 use the highest-fidelity tree with ≤ 15 leaves. That is effectively a second tuning knob — an explicit complexity budget driven by “fits on a slide” rather than by cross-validation — and it is honest to say so. The surrogate’s whole purpose is to be read, not just to win a fidelity contest; the realised cost is ~0.009 fidelity on Lexical and ~0.003 on FullLite, which is a fair price for a diagram a reviewer can follow branch-by-branch.

Per-class fidelity — the Sens/Spec decomposition. Overall fidelity averages over both classes, but the rest of this report (§6.1.1, §8) treats the two error types separately because they have asymmetric cost in the proxy setting. The surrogate inherits that framing: we also report Sens vs RF (the share of RF-called phishing the tree also calls phishing) and Spec vs RF (the share of RF-called legit the tree also calls legit). On both tiers these two agreement numbers land within a few tenths of a percent of each other — Lexical 0.957 / 0.962 (diff 0.005), FullLite 0.987 / 0.985 (diff 0.002) — the surrogate tracks RF symmetrically in both directions, not by picking up the easy class. If Sens-vs-RF and Spec-vs-RF had diverged substantially, it would mean the tree copied RF well on one class and not on the other — a warning that the “visualisation” was hiding a class-specific failure mode. They don’t, so the visualisation is balanced.

The depth saturation table is worth re-reading with that in mind: at depth 3 on Lexical the surrogate’s Spec vs RF collapses to ~0.87 while Sens vs RF stays at ~0.98 — an 11 pp asymmetry that means the shallow tree only copies RF on the phish side. The asymmetry shrinks monotonically with depth and is gone by depth 6–7 (~0.97 on both axes). That is the empirical justification for picking depth ≥ 5 as the readability floor, separate from the fidelity argument: shallower diagrams visualise only half of the RF’s behaviour.

Deployment operating point — and where the single-tree cost shows up. The deployment-level columns (tree_sens vs. rf_sens, tree_spec vs. rf_spec) ask a different question: if a reader replaces the RF with the surrogate in production, how much does the operating point shift? On FullLite the answer is “barely” — Sens shifts from 0.997 to 0.986 (1.1 pp), Spec from 0.996 to 0.983 (1.3 pp), both inside the 2 pp tolerance we set as the bar for a faithful visualisation. On Lexical the picture is less rosy: Spec is preserved (0.743 → 0.733, 1.0 pp) but Sens drops by 3.3 pp (0.968 → 0.934). That gap is genuine, not a tuning artefact — it is the single-tree cost that §6.2 motivated: Lexical is the tier where RF beats every parametric model by ~0.10 AUC precisely because the ensemble averages partial 3+-feature interactions a single rpart tree cannot encode. The same effect now shows up as a 0.107 gap between Tree AUC (0.862) and RF AUC (0.969), and as those 3.3 pp lost Sens. We name it explicitly rather than smoothing it over: the Lexical surrogate is a faithful structural visualisation of RF — same root, same feature ordering as varImpPlot — but it is not an operational replacement for RF on Lexical, and that is exactly the finding Task 4 is supposed to surface. On FullLite, where the RF lead over single-decision rules has already evaporated, the surrogate is both faithful and operationally close.

FullLite sanity check. On FullLite every decent model sits at AUC ≈ 0.999 (§6.1), so a tree of any non-trivial depth trivially matches the RF’s output. Fidelity on FullLite therefore carries very little information about the RF — it mostly confirms that the grid search and caching pipeline are unbiased. The interesting tier is Lexical: that’s where the RF actually wins a non-trivial contest and the question “is this a one-tree decision rule in disguise, or does the ensemble really buy us something?” has a non-obvious answer.

8. Conclusion — H1 gradient

  1. Non-parametric > parametric when the feature tier is weak. On the Lexical tier the non-parametric advantage is the largest of any tier, and every single CV fold shows the same order. This is exactly the regime H1 targets: URL counts interact in non-linear ways (digit runs × length × subdomain depth), and linear or Gaussian decision boundaries just can’t capture those interactions. Read the exact diff off §6.2.
  2. Trust also favours non-parametric, but only barely. With only seven features — most of them plain binary flags — there’s little non-linear structure left for non-parametric models to discover.
  3. Behavior is the honest middle tier. With the six near-leaky page counts (LineOfCode, NoOfExternalRef, NoOfImage, NoOfSelfRef, NoOfJS, NoOfCSS) stripped out — each of which individually gives univariate AUC ≥ 0.95 and single-handedly solves the task — the remaining 14 Behavior features are a realistic page-level signal without saturation. This is where we can read the family contrast cleanly at medium signal strength.
  4. FullLite is the honest > 30-feature benchmark. It meets the assignment’s “several dozen parameters” rule without relying on features that single-handedly solve the task. Even so, 34 non-leaky features are already enough to make every model cluster near the ceiling — the family contrast on FullLite tells us whether non-parametric flexibility still buys anything in a realistic high-dimensional setting, or whether the task is simply resolved by that point.

Train–test gap check. The per-model tables in §4–§5 also report Train AUC and the Gap = Train − Test. Parametric models (LR-Ridge, LDA, NB) sit within ~0.01 of their test AUC on every tier — they cannot memorise 24 k rows with so few effective parameters, so their CV scores are trustworthy. Non-parametric models show a larger in-bag fit (Random Forest and KNN approach Train AUC ≈ 1.0 by construction), but their CV ROC and Test AUC still agree tightly — the out-of-sample numbers reported above are not inflated by leakage. In other words, the H1 gradient is measured on generalisation performance, not on any model’s ability to memorise the training set.

Threshold-0.5 quality (Precision / Sensitivity / Specificity). The §6.1.1 table complements the AUC ranking with operating-point numbers. Two findings only visible once Precision is reported:

  • SVM-RBF on Lexical is not only the AUC winner but also the only model that is simultaneously high on all three (Prec ≈ Sens ≈ Spec ≈ 0.98). Every other model on Lexical trades one for another.
  • Naive Bayes mirrors itself across tiers. On Lexical it over-calls phish (Sens 0.999, Prec 0.618) — a high-recall / low-precision flood. On Behavior the same independence violation flips the other way (Sens 0.732, Prec 0.995) — almost never calls phish, but when it does, it is right. Both are symptoms of the same broken independence assumption; the direction of the miscalibration is tier-dependent.

This is the part AUC hides: two models with AUC ≈ 0.99 on Behavior can still have fundamentally different operating-point behaviour, and the Precision/Sensitivity decomposition is what makes that visible. Brought back to the corporate-proxy framing of EDA §1.1: the proxy doesn’t consume an AUC, it consumes a single block/allow verdict per click. Two models that look tied on the ranking metric can produce very different proxies — one that floods the helpdesk with false blocks, and one that silently lets phish through. AUC treats these as equivalent; the deployment doesn’t.

Practical takeaway. A URL-only first-line phishing detector gains a lot from a non-parametric model (SVM-RBF or KNN gain ~0.10 AUC over LR / NB on Lexical, and SVM additionally keeps Precision and Recall balanced at ~0.98). Once the system can afford to fetch the page and compute Behavior-level features, the AUC choice of model becomes almost irrelevant and simpler, more explainable models (LR, LDA) are good enough — though the operating-point choice (Precision-first vs. Recall-first) should still be driven by the cost of a false block vs. a missed phish.

8.1 Selected model

The model we pick for the corporate-proxy deployment is SVM-RBF.

  1. On Lexical — the only tier the proxy actually sees before the page loads — SVM-RBF posts AUC 0.995 with Sens 0.985 / Spec 0.983. Every other model either drops ≥ 0.10 AUC or trades one error type for another at threshold 0.5.
  2. It is symmetric in both error directions without recalibration. At the default 0.5 cut the miss rate (~1.7 %) and the false-block rate (~1.7 %) are essentially equal — no threshold tuning needed to make the operating point deployable. LR, LDA, NB and RF would all need a separate calibration step on Lexical before they could go behind a proxy; SVM-RBF does not.
  3. It holds the quality on every other tier too. Trust, Behavior and FullLite all land in the Sens ≈ Spec ≈ 0.97–1.00 region, so the same model works if the proxy ever expands beyond URL-only — no model swap needed when more signal becomes available.
  4. Inference cost is acceptable for the proxy loop. KNN matches SVM-RBF on Lexical AUC (0.987 vs. 0.995) but requires ~24 k distance computations per click, which is exactly the “cheap before the page loads” constraint from our hypothesis.

When we would revisit the choice. If the proxy pipeline later gains Behavior- or FullLite-grade signals and explainability becomes a requirement, LR-Ridge or LDA on that richer tier is a defensible downgrade — both reach AUC ≥ 0.99 there with balanced Sens/Spec, and their coefficients are interpretable. But for the URL-only first-line filter that out hypothesis is built around, SVM-RBF is the only model whose numbers support the deployment directly.
